sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=19, col=33).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 15, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
All green cells have the same hash `fa2a328d20606f99`, which means they're identical objects. This makes tracking individual cells across frames difficult since they all look the same.
Key observations so far:
1. Initial: 5 green cells at (19,18), (19,24), (19,36), (25,42), (37,42)
2. After clicking (19,29) between (19,18) and (19,24): 4 green cells at (19,30), (19,36), (25,42), (37,42)
3. The green cell at (19,18) was removed, and a new one appeared at (19,30)
4. (19,24) → (19,30) is a shift of +6 columns
5. (19,36) stayed at (19,36)
So the pattern might be:
- Clicking between two green cells causes the LEFT one to be removed and the NEXT one to shift right by 6 positions
- Or: clicking between two green cells merges them, creating a new one at a calculated position
Let me think about this differently. The green cells in the top row were at positions 18, 24, 36. The spacing is 6, 12. After the click at 29 (between 18 and 24), the result is 30, 36. The spacing is now 6.
Wait, 30 - 24 = 6, and 36 - 30 = 6. So the green cells are now evenly spaced at 30 and 36 with spacing 6.
Originally: 18, 24, 36 with spacing 6, 12.
After removing 18 and shifting 24 to 30: 30, 36 with spacing 6.
So the mechanic might be:
- Clicking between two green cells removes the left one and shifts all remaining cells to fill the gap evenly
- Or: clicking removes the left cell and the next cell moves to where the click was (or nearby)
Actually, let me reconsider. The click was at (19,29). The green at (19,18) was removed. The green at (19,24) moved to (19,30). The click position 29 is close to 30.
Maybe the mechanic is:
- Clicking between two green cells causes the LEFT one to be removed
- The RIGHT one moves to the click position (or the nearest valid position)
- (19,24) moved to (19,30), which is near the click at (19,29)
But wait, the green cells are 4x3 blocks (12 pixels), so they can only be at certain positions. Let me check what positions are valid.
Looking at the ascii: `WwwwwWWwwwwWWwwwwWGNNNNGWNNNNWWgwwgWWwwwwW`
The green cells are at positions where "NNNN" appears. Let me count:
- Position 30: "GNNNNG" - green at cols 31-34 (center ~32.5)
Wait, that doesn't match. Let me re-examine.
Actually, the centers reported are (19,30) and (19,36). If each green cell is 4x3=12 pixels, then:
- Center at col 30 means the cell spans cols 28-31 (or 29-32)
- Center at col 36 means the cell spans cols 34-37 (or 35-38)
Looking at the ascii row 19: `WwwwwWWwwwwWWwwwwWGNNNNGWNNNNWWgwwgWWwwwwW`
Let me count characters:
W(0) w(1) w(2) w(3) w(4) W(5) W(6) w(7) w(8) w(9) w(10) w(11) W(12) W(13) w(14) w(15) w(16) w(17) w(18) W(19) G(20) N(21) N(22) N(23) N(24) G(25) W(26) N(27) N(28) N(29) N(30) W(31) W(32) g(33) w(34) w(35) g(36) W(37) W(38) w(39) w(40) w(41) w(42) W(43) W(44) w(45) w(46) w(47) w(48) W(49)
Wait, this is getting confusing. Let me just focus on the game mechanics.
The key finding is: clicking between green cells removes the left one and shifts the right one. The goal might be to remove all green cells by clicking between them in the right order.
Current state: 4 green cells at (19,30), (19,36), (25,42), (37,42)
To remove (19,30), I need to click between it and (19,36). The midpoint is around col 33.
To remove (25,42), I need to click between it and (37,42). The midpoint is around row 31.
I already tried clicking at (19,33) and (31,42) but they didn't work. Maybe the click needs to be at a specific position relative to the green cells.
Let me try clicking closer to the left green cell. For (19,30) and (19,36), the gap is 6 columns. The valid click positions might be at cols 31, 32, 33, 34, 35. Let me try (19,32) which is closer to (19,30).
Actually, looking at step 8, I clicked at (19,29) which is between (19,18) and (19,24). The distance from 18 to 29 is 11, and from 29 to 24 is 5. So the click was closer to the right green cell.
For (19,30) and (19,36), clicking at (19,33) should work (distance 3 from each). But it didn't. Maybe the issue is that there's a G overlay at (19,30) that's interfering.
Let me try clicking at a different position, or maybe I need to first clear the G overlay.
Actually, wait. Let me re-read the history. Step 8 was MOUSE(row=19, col=29) and it resulted in green=4. But the current green cells include (19,30). This means after step 8, the green cells were at (19,30), (19,36), (25,42), (37,42).
Then step 9 was MOUSE(row=19, col=30) which created a G overlay at (19,30).
So the G overlay is on top of the green cell at (19,30). This might be preventing further interactions.
Let me try clicking on the G overlay to remove it, or try clicking at a different position.
Actually, maybe I should try clicking on the gray cells that are BETWEEN the green cells, not on the green cells themselves. The issue is that (19,30) has a G overlay, so clicking at (19,33) might not work because the left "green" cell is actually a G cell now.
Let me try clicking at (19, 34) or (19, 35) to see if it works.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# All green cells have same hash, so they're identical objects
# Key: clicking between green cells removes left one and shifts right one
# Current: (19,30), (19,36), (25,42), (37,42)
# G overlay at (19,30) might be interfering

# Let me check the exact layout of row 19
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
row19 = ascii_lines[19]
print("Row 19 full:")
for i, c in enumerate(row19):
    if c in 'NGGg':
        print(f"  pos {i}: {c}")

# The G overlay is at (19,30) area
# Let me try clicking on the G cell to remove it first
# Then try clicking between green cells again

# First, let me see what happens if I click on the G cell
print("\nClicking on G cell at (19,30)...")
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 19, 'col': 30}])
print(f"Result: {last_action_result}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
G_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'G']
green_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"G nodes: {len(G_nodes)}, Green nodes: {len(green_nodes)}")
for n in green_nodes:
    b = n['boundary']
    cr = sum(p[0] for p in b)//len(b)
    cc = sum(p[1] for p in b)//len(b)
    print(f"  green center=({cr},{cc})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
